author: potuz
content: I thought <@1126229231572094976> was going to bring this up today, was surprised not to see it in the agenda

author: nero_eth
content: Hmm I wasn't sure we've yet reached a conclusion. But now you saying that, yeah,  probably a missed opportunity to make quick progress.

author: nero_eth
content:
I guess, for now, the best thing to do would be the first point, not downloading and serving payloads on the CL, deduplicating data on the two layers.
What is meant with top-up sync here? Is this the mode where the CL would provide all payloads for the EL, just like we progress the chain at the head?

author: nero_eth
content: I guess helping syncing peers in top-up sync,  clients could still request payloads from their local EL and serve it to peers that do different sync, e.g. what nimbus does?

author: arnetheduck
content: yes, top-up sync is https://hackmd.io/siEIwl9QTlODSZ6DYH3iqg , ie the _possiblity_ for the CL to sync the EL simply by feeding it payload by payload one by one (from the point of view of the EL, it "looks like" the blocks are arriving from gossip)

author: arnetheduck
content: https://github.com/status-im/nimbus-eth1/blob/master/nrpc/nrpc.nim - this is a small demo application that connects to the execution api _and_ the beacon api - whenever the CL claims to have blocks that the EL doesn't have, it queries the CL for those blocks and drip-feeds them to the EL - if you have a working EL-CL setup, you can for example use this script to drive a second EL from the first EL-CL pair - of course, this is mostly a proof-of-concept that we use for testing, but it's actually pretty useful - it works with any CL/EL (not just nimbus) as long as they conform to the execution/beacon api specs

author: arnetheduck
content: assuming the second EL has a recent-enough state (no snap sync) and is not used for block-building (due to mempool), it can stay entirely offline (for example, you can use it for your wallet)
